question: Rather than just naming a strength, can you tell us about a moment when that strength actually made a difference?
lookingFor: A concrete example where a genuine strength shifted an outcome — not a label bolted onto a vague story.

bullets: Name the strength and drop straight into a specific situation
Show the before-and-after: what was at stake and what changed because of you
Reflect briefly on whether that strength has limits or costs
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My ability to break complicated things into steps is probably my most consistent strength. When our extended project supervisor left mid-year and the group was lost, I put together a one-page plan that split the remaining work into weekly targets we could actually track. We went from zero clarity to submitting on time with strong marks. That said, I've learned that my impulse to structure everything can frustrate people who think better by talking it out first, so I try to build in space for that now before I move into planning mode.

commonMistakes: Naming a strength without a story to back it
Picking an example so small it doesn't illustrate the point
Showing no awareness of the strength's limits
followUps: Has that strength ever caused friction with someone who works differently?

question: What's something you only recently realised was a weakness — something you didn't see before someone pointed it out?
lookingFor: The capacity to receive feedback and genuinely update your self-image — not a polished weakness-is-a-strength pivot.

bullets: Describe the feedback honestly — what was said and by whom
Acknowledge the initial reaction: defensiveness is human and worth naming
Show a genuine shift in how you see yourself and what changed
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A teacher told me last year that I tend to talk over people when I'm excited, and I genuinely didn't believe her at first. I thought I was just contributing. But once she said it I started noticing it — particularly in group discussions, I'd jump in before someone had finished. It was uncomfortable to sit with that. I started making a conscious effort to wait for a full stop before speaking, and a teammate actually commented that I'd become much easier to work with. I wouldn't have found that without being told directly.

commonMistakes: Choosing a weakness you've magically already resolved
Dismissing or qualifying the feedback rather than engaging with it
Giving a rehearsed answer that sounds too clean
followUps: How do you now actively seek feedback you might not hear otherwise?

question: Can you think of a time when one of your strengths actually worked against you?
lookingFor: Sophisticated self-awareness — the understanding that any real strength has a shadow side when over-applied.

bullets: Name the strength without framing it as a secret weakness
Describe a genuine situation where it created a problem
Show that you've adjusted your approach rather than abandoning the strength
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm thorough — I read everything carefully and rarely submit work I'm not satisfied with. But during a group project I kept pushing for more checks before we went live, and what felt like quality control to me felt like a lack of trust to my teammates. We nearly missed the deadline and two people said I was making them feel their work wasn't good enough. I had to learn to distinguish situations where thoroughness adds real value from situations where it's just me managing my own anxiety, and to say that difference out loud to the people I work with.

commonMistakes: Turning it into an implicit brag with no real cost
Choosing a trivial example that doesn't feel genuine
Failing to explain what you changed going forward
followUps: How do you decide now when to apply that strength fully versus hold it back?

question: How do you tend to behave when you're under real pressure, and how do you know that about yourself?
lookingFor: Honest, evidence-based self-knowledge about behaviour under stress — not a theoretical answer.

bullets: Describe a specific pattern, not a general claim
Anchor it in real observation — exams, deadlines, a crisis
Name one thing you do to manage that pattern
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Under real pressure I go quiet and get very task-focused, which looks like calm from the outside but can come across as cold or distant. I noticed it when a friend told me I'd barely spoken to her during a particularly hard exam period and she'd assumed she'd done something wrong. I've learned to give people a heads-up when I'm in that mode — a quick message saying I'm in deadline crunch and will be quieter than usual. It saves relationships and lets me work the way I actually work, without others filling in the gap with the wrong story.

commonMistakes: Describing an idealised version of yourself rather than the real one
No evidence — just a claim with no grounding
Making it sound like you have everything perfectly managed
followUps: What's the earliest sign that tells you pressure is affecting you?

question: When you look at other applicants with similar grades to yours, what do you think genuinely sets you apart?
lookingFor: Confident self-assessment without arrogance — the ability to articulate a distinctive quality that isn't just academic performance.

bullets: Name something specific, not a generic virtue like 'hardworking'
Back it with a concrete example or pattern
Stay confident without disparaging others
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think it's the combination of curiosity and follow-through. A lot of people get interested in something and move on — I tend to chase it down. When I got interested in the ethics of AI last year, I didn't just watch a documentary; I cold-emailed a researcher whose paper I'd read, got a call with her, and ended up helping organise a school panel on it. That drive to go from curious to doing something seems to be unusual enough that people comment on it. I can't claim it'll always look that dramatic, but it's a consistent pattern in how I work.

commonMistakes: Answering with qualities that would describe most strong students
Being so modest there's no clear answer
Implying other candidates are lacking
followUps: Give me another example where that same quality showed up.

question: What conditions bring out your best academic work — and what gets in the way?
lookingFor: Concrete self-knowledge about learning conditions, not a generic 'I work hard' answer.

bullets: Be specific about what helps — environment, timing, process
Be equally honest about what doesn't work
Explain what you do when the ideal conditions aren't available
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I do my best thinking early in the morning with no music on and a rough outline already in front of me — if I start cold I spin my wheels. What gets in the way is open-ended time with no anchor; I tend to procrastinate when I can't see where a piece of work ends. So when a deadline is far away I break it into smaller self-set checkpoints with shorter windows. That sounds simple but it genuinely doubled my output in the second half of last year. I'm going into university knowing I'll need to build that structure deliberately.

commonMistakes: Describing ideal conditions with no acknowledgment of the flip side
Conditions so generic they give no real insight
No evidence that you've actually adapted to non-ideal situations
followUps: How will you build those conditions for yourself at university, where your schedule won't be set for you?

question: Has there been a period where you genuinely doubted whether you were good enough for the path you're on? How did you come through it?
lookingFor: Authentic reflection on self-doubt — evidence of emotional honesty and a real coping process, not a polished 'I overcame it' arc.

bullets: Name the doubt clearly and what triggered it
Describe what you actually did — not what you should have done
Show what you carry forward from the experience
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: When my predicted grades came in lower than I'd expected, I genuinely questioned whether I was cut out for this. I spent about three weeks applying to different courses — it was partly sensible planning and partly panicking. What brought me back was reading an essay by a researcher I admire who described failing her first year and taking a detour through a subject she hated before finding her direction. It didn't fix anything, but it reminded me that difficulty isn't disqualification. I went back to my tutor, asked what was going wrong, and found out two of my marks had been dragged down by the same organisational mistake I could actually fix. That grounded me.

commonMistakes: Presenting self-doubt as something that was quickly and easily resolved
Avoiding the emotional reality to look more composed
A story with no specific turning point
followUps: What would you tell a classmate going through that same doubt right now?

question: Tell me about a time you held a position that was unpopular with people around you. What made you stick with it?
lookingFor: Evidence that values and reasoning, not social pressure, guide the applicant's decisions — without self-righteousness.

bullets: Describe the position and the social cost honestly
Explain the reasoning that kept you there — not just 'I knew I was right'
Acknowledge any uncertainty or discomfort you felt
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Our form group was voting to endorse a school policy I thought was unfair to a small group of students who weren't in the room. Most people were fine with it and wanted to move on. I raised an objection and it made the meeting awkward — a few people were clearly frustrated that I was slowing things down. I wasn't certain I was right, but I felt we hadn't asked the people most affected, and I said so. We ended up tabling it to get more input, and the final policy was different and better. I wasn't popular for thirty minutes. What kept me going was less conviction and more the discomfort of knowing I'd stayed quiet to be liked.

commonMistakes: Choosing an example where you were obviously and unanimously vindicated
Sounding self-righteous about your own courage
Describing pressure as a minor inconvenience rather than a real challenge
followUps: Was there ever a time you held a position, stood by it, and turned out to be wrong?

question: What's something you've genuinely got better at over the last two years, and how did you get there?
lookingFor: A real, traceable arc of improvement — evidence that the applicant can identify and close gaps through deliberate effort.

bullets: Name the specific skill or quality, not a vague 'I've grown'
Explain what you actually did to improve — not just 'I worked harder'
Show what changed and how you know it changed
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Public speaking was genuinely painful for me two years ago — I'd avoid any task that involved it. I started small: I volunteered to give a three-minute presentation at a club meeting when I didn't have to. It went badly. I watched the recording, which was uncomfortable, and figured out my specific problems: rushing and looking at the ceiling. I targeted those specifically over the next few months. By last term I presented at an inter-school debate in front of three hundred people and felt in control. I know I've improved because I no longer plan my schedule around avoiding it.

commonMistakes: Describing passive improvement — 'I just got better over time'
Choosing something trivial that doesn't reveal much
No concrete marker of how you know you've improved
followUps: What's the next skill you're planning to deliberately work on?

question: Is there something about your background or life experience that has shaped how you think, in a way that isn't obvious from your application?
lookingFor: A distinctive perspective that enriches the cohort — expressed with specificity rather than vague diversity-speak.

bullets: Name something concrete and true, not a generalisation about your community
Explain the specific effect it has on how you think or approach problems
Connect it to how it would contribute to the course or university
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I grew up in a house where we moved a lot — eight schools before I was sixteen. I never had the luxury of building expertise through the same teachers or the same curriculum, so I learned to orient quickly in new environments and pick out what actually mattered versus what was just context. That's made me very good at reading a room, at asking the question that moves a stuck conversation forward, and at not getting attached to how I learned something — only to whether I actually understand it. I think that's shaped me as a thinker more than any single subject has.

commonMistakes: A generic answer about 'coming from a humble background' with no specifics
Framing it as disadvantage overcome rather than perspective gained
Telling the panel what they want to hear about diversity
followUps: How do you think that perspective will show up in seminars or group work at university?

question: If the people who know you best were describing you honestly — not flatteringly — what would they say is your biggest shortcoming?
lookingFor: Self-awareness grounded in others' perceptions — the ability to hold a realistic picture of yourself rather than a curated one.

bullets: Name something real that people around you have actually observed
Acknowledge it without either dismissing it or over-apologising
Show you're actively working on it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My closest friends would probably say I can be frustrating to make plans with because I overthink the options too long before committing. It's connected to something real — I genuinely want to pick the best path — but it can read as flakiness or indecisiveness, which isn't fair to people who need a clear answer. I've been actively practising setting a personal deadline for decisions: I give myself one round of research and then commit, even if I'm not completely certain. It's helped, though I still notice the pull to keep weighing options longer than I should.

commonMistakes: Guessing what the panel wants to hear rather than what's actually true
Choosing a flaw so mild it says nothing
Framing it as other people misunderstanding you
followUps: Has anyone told you something about yourself that you initially disagreed with and later came to accept?

question: Do you think of yourself as a leader — and whether you do or don't, what's your honest read on why?
lookingFor: A nuanced understanding of where the applicant adds value in groups — not a yes/no and not an automatic leadership claim.

bullets: Answer the question directly and honestly
Back it up with real experience of how you function in groups
Show you understand that leadership takes different forms
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Sometimes, but not always, and I think that's actually accurate. In situations with a clear problem to solve and ambiguity about who should drive it, I tend to step into that space naturally. But I'm not someone who needs to be in charge, and I've noticed I can contribute more in certain groups by being the person who gives the leader honest feedback they're not getting from others. I had a year where I was vice-captain of the sports team and the captain was more effective because I was willing to disagree with her privately rather than front it out in public. I think knowing when to lead and when to support is more useful than just always defaulting to the front.

commonMistakes: Automatically claiming leadership with no self-questioning
Deflecting entirely: 'I'm not really a leader'
Confusing holding a leadership title with actually leading
followUps: Tell me about a time you chose to follow when you could have led. What made you make that call?

question: Has there been a moment where you got something badly wrong because you overestimated your own ability? What happened?
lookingFor: Genuine humility and honest reflection — not a reframed success story, but a real account of overconfidence and its consequences.

bullets: Describe the misjudgement specifically, not vaguely
Explain why you overestimated — what convinced you at the time
Show what you now do differently as a result
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I took on organising a fundraising event without having done it before, and I told the group I had it covered because I'd helped with smaller things and thought it was the same at scale. It wasn't. I'd underestimated the coordination involved and ended up missing a venue deadline that cost us the booking. The group had to scramble. I learned the difference between being capable and being ready — and since then I ask myself specifically what experience I have for this exact scale of thing, not just whether I'm good at adjacent things. It's a slower question to ask, but it's caught me twice since.

commonMistakes: Framing overconfidence as a positive ('I believed in myself')
A story where the consequences were trivial
Blaming others for what followed
followUps: How do you calibrate your confidence level before committing to something now?

question: How would you describe the way you think through a complex problem — not the answer you reach, but the process?
lookingFor: Genuine metacognition — a student who has observed their own thinking and can describe it specifically.

bullets: Describe an actual pattern, not an aspirational one
Use a concrete example to make it tangible
Acknowledge where the process sometimes breaks down
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I almost always need to make the problem physical before I can think about it clearly — I write it out by hand, even if I'm going to type the final answer. Something about the slower pace of writing makes me notice assumptions I'd otherwise skip past. I'll often get halfway through writing and realise I've framed the problem wrongly, which is annoying but useful. The place it breaks down is when there's time pressure — I try to skip straight to the answer and I usually regret it. For exams I've started leaving the first two minutes just to read and write the question back in my own words, which feels like wasted time but almost always saves me more time on the other end.

commonMistakes: Describing an idealised process with no acknowledgment of when it fails
Being so abstract it sounds like you've never actually watched yourself think
Confusing process with outcome
followUps: Can you show me what you mean with a real problem you've worked through recently?

question: Looking at what you've achieved so far — how much of it do you put down to ability, and how much to effort or circumstance?
lookingFor: An honest, non-defensive appraisal of what's driven success — neither false modesty nor unexamined confidence.

bullets: Give an honest split, not a diplomatic non-answer
Acknowledge the role of factors outside your control
Connect that honesty to how you approach challenges going forward
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Honestly, more effort than raw ability — and more fortunate circumstances than I sometimes admit. I had a biology teacher in Year 11 who saw something in me before I did and put me in for a competition I would never have entered myself. That changed a lot. My ability is real but it's not exceptional — what I've built on is consistency and being willing to do the unglamorous work. I think that's actually made me more resilient than people who find things come easily, because I've never had the option of coasting. I go into university knowing I'll need to keep working hard, and I'm comfortable with that.

commonMistakes: Attributing everything to hard work with no acknowledgment of advantage
False modesty that dismisses genuine ability
An answer with no real self-examination
followUps: Is there an area where you know you've been relying on ability without putting in the work?

question: Are you the same person in the classroom as you are outside it? Where do you notice the gap, if there is one?
lookingFor: Perceptive honesty about context-dependent behaviour — the kind of awareness that distinguishes a reflective applicant from a polished one.

bullets: Engage genuinely with the premise rather than claiming total consistency
Name a real context where a different side emerges
Show that you've thought about what that gap means
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Mostly yes, but not entirely. In class I'm fairly measured — I take time before I speak and I tend to qualify things. Outside of class, especially with people I'm comfortable with, I'm quicker, more irreverent, and I push ideas harder before I've checked whether they make sense. I don't think either version is false, but the classroom version is edited and the outside version is rawer. I've been thinking about whether I leave too much of the raw thinking in the classroom — whether being careful has become a habit of holding back rather than actually checking my reasoning. I'm not sure yet.

commonMistakes: Claiming to be exactly the same everywhere — it reads as unexamined
Describing a gap but offering no reflection on what it means
Making the gap sound like something to be embarrassed about
followUps: Which version of yourself tends to do better thinking, and why?

question: Tell me about a time you received criticism that was hard to hear. What did you do with it?
lookingFor: Evidence of psychological maturity — the ability to sit with uncomfortable feedback and choose a constructive response.

bullets: Name the criticism specifically — not a euphemised version
Describe your honest first reaction, including any defensiveness
Show the deliberate choice to use it rather than dismiss it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My form tutor told me that I frequently dominated group discussions and that other students had stopped contributing when I was in the room. That landed badly — I thought I was helping by driving conversations forward. My first instinct was to explain why that wasn't the whole picture. I sat with it instead and started watching how I behaved in the next few sessions, and I had to admit she was right. I began tracking how often I spoke versus how often others did, and started asking questions instead of making points. Within a month another teacher commented unsolicited that the group dynamic had improved. I needed the feedback to see something I couldn't see myself.

commonMistakes: Choosing criticism that was obviously fair and easy to accept
Skipping over the defensive reaction
Ending on what you learned with no evidence it actually changed behaviour
followUps: Is there feedback you've received that you still disagree with?

question: What's the most intellectually difficult thing you've tried to understand, and where did you get stuck?
lookingFor: Intellectual courage and honesty — the ability to engage with the limits of your own understanding without retreating to comfortable territory.

bullets: Name something genuinely difficult — not something you've fully mastered
Describe the specific point where comprehension broke down
Show what you did next: pressed on, sought help, changed approach
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Trying to properly understand quantum entanglement — not the metaphor, but the actual mathematics. I got to a point in an online lecture series where the linear algebra made sense but I couldn't bridge it to what the physics was describing. The vectors were fine but 'superposition' kept slipping when I tried to hold it as a physical claim rather than a mathematical one. I stopped, bought a more accessible book, built up the conceptual picture first, and went back to the maths after that. I still don't think I fully have it — which is part of why I want to study physics, not a reason to pretend I understand it already.

commonMistakes: Choosing a topic you've already solved — it defeats the question
Describing confusion with no description of what you tried
Retreating to 'it was hard but I got through it' without specifics
followUps: What does not understanding something feel like to you — and how do you decide whether to push through or step back?

question: When you're working in a team, what role do you tend to naturally fall into — and is that always the role where you add the most value?
lookingFor: Honest self-knowledge about group dynamics — the ability to distinguish habitual role from most effective role.

bullets: Name the habitual role clearly
Give an example where that role served the group well
Show honest awareness of a situation where a different role would have served better
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I tend to become the person who synthesises — I'll listen to an argument, pull out the common ground, and summarise where we are. It helps groups move forward when they're stuck, and I've seen it work well. But there are times when what a group needs is someone to push back hard on a flawed plan, and I notice I soften disagreement to preserve momentum. That's not always right. I'm more valuable when I learn to sit with a productive argument rather than rushing to resolve it. I'm working on reading the room for which mode the group actually needs, not just the one I'm most comfortable in.

commonMistakes: Naming a role you perform rather than where you genuinely thrive
Claiming you adapt perfectly to whatever the group needs
No acknowledgment of where the default role has limits
followUps: Can you tell me about a time when you deliberately stepped outside your usual role in a group?

question: When no one is watching and there's no grade attached, what kinds of things do you find yourself pursuing anyway — and what does that tell you about yourself?
lookingFor: Evidence of intrinsic motivation and intellectual personality — what the applicant is like when external incentives are removed.

bullets: Name something specific, not 'I read a lot'
Connect it to a genuine curiosity or value rather than a strategic interest
Show some self-reflection on what that pattern reveals
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been building small data tools for about two years now — mostly things that do one job better than a spreadsheet would. None of them are impressive and most don't get used much beyond me. I just like the problem of working out the neatest way to make something work. I think it tells me that I'm genuinely motivated by designing systems rather than by getting credit for them — which is useful to know. It also tells me I can sustain effort on something with no external reward for a long time, as long as the problem feels worth solving. That's the kind of motivation I want to point at something that matters.

commonMistakes: Listing hobbies without any reflection on what they reveal
Naming something that happens to align with the application and sounds strategic
Shallow self-analysis: 'it tells me I'm passionate'
followUps: Has anything you've pursued purely for interest crossed over into your studies or ambitions?

question: If you could be more honest about your application than most students dare to be — what's the part of your profile you're least proud of, and why?
lookingFor: The courage to be genuinely candid about a real gap, combined with context and forward intention — not a performed confession.

bullets: Name something real, not a cosmetic imperfection
Give honest context — what happened, not just what it looks like
End on what you've taken from it or what you're doing differently
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My Year 11 attendance record has a gap I've never fully explained on paper. My mother was seriously ill for four months and I was picking up responsibilities at home, and I made the decision to prioritise that over school in a way that affected my grades and my participation. I'm not ashamed of the choice — it was the right one. But I'm aware it shows up as an inconsistency in an otherwise solid record and I haven't always had the confidence to explain it directly rather than just hoping no one asks. I'm telling you now because it shaped how I see priorities, and I think it's relevant to who I am.

commonMistakes: Naming a gap that's already been explained elsewhere and isn't revealing
Treating it as a confessional rather than a reflective moment
Framing the answer in a way that seeks sympathy rather than understanding
followUps: Is there anything in your application you think would look different if the panel understood more context?

question: When you receive praise for something, how do you know whether it's deserved — and what do you do when you're not sure?
lookingFor: Calibrated confidence — the ability to assess quality independently rather than deferring entirely to external validation.

bullets: Show you have internal standards, not just external feedback
Describe a real moment when praise felt unearned and how you handled it
Avoid both false modesty and uncritical acceptance
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I try to check praise against my own sense of the work before I accept it. When a teacher called an essay my best yet, my first thought was whether I agreed — and I wasn't sure I did. It felt more polished than previous essays but I'd cut an argument that I thought was the most interesting part because it was underdeveloped. I went back to that argument, developed it, and submitted a revised version for a different context. The praise was kind but it told me something about what was visible to others versus what I thought the real problem was. I find that gap more useful than the praise itself.

commonMistakes: Accepting all praise at face value with no internal check
Dismissing all praise as unearned — that's not calibration, it's deflection
An answer that never gets to a concrete example
followUps: Is there praise you received that you now, in hindsight, think was actually correct?

question: If you had to identify one pattern in how you operate — something that keeps showing up across different areas of your life — what would it be?
lookingFor: Deep self-observation and the ability to draw meaningful patterns across different domains rather than situational self-description.

bullets: Name a genuine recurring pattern, not a virtue
Show it appearing in at least two different contexts
Reflect honestly on whether that pattern is a strength, a liability, or both
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I have a pattern of starting things at the last acceptable moment — not the last possible moment, but later than most people would be comfortable with. It shows up in how I study, how I start projects, even in social plans. Part of it is that I work well under a certain kind of focused pressure, and I know it. The problem is that when something goes wrong late in the process — a source I can't access, a piece of research that contradicts my argument — there's no slack. I've had it go wrong twice badly enough that I've had to build artificial earlier deadlines into my system, just to create the buffer the real deadline won't give me. It's a pattern I understand and work around, not one I've beaten.

commonMistakes: Naming a quality rather than a behavioural pattern
A pattern that only shows up in one context
Treating it as either purely positive or purely negative with no nuance
followUps: Can you think of a context where that pattern has actually been an advantage?

question: Are you more confident than you are competent, or more competent than you are confident — and can you give me evidence for your answer?
lookingFor: Sharp self-analysis with genuine honesty — the ability to hold the tension between confidence and actual performance with evidence.

bullets: Give a direct, committed answer to the question
Provide concrete evidence, not just a feeling
Acknowledge where the balance shifts depending on context
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In most areas, more competent than confident — and I can actually point to evidence. My extended essay was marked in the top tier in the school but I submitted it expecting middling feedback. I consistently underestimate my written work while overestimating how prepared I am to speak in public, which is the reverse pattern. So it's not consistent across domains. In writing I trust myself less than I should; in speaking I've sometimes overestimated how ready I was and it's shown. I find the asymmetry interesting — it suggests my self-assessments are domain-specific and probably worth calibrating separately rather than with a single global adjustment.

commonMistakes: Claiming the more modest answer without evidence
Giving a diplomatic non-answer: 'it depends'
Treating the question as if it has one right answer
followUps: What's the practical implication of that for how you approach unfamiliar challenges?

question: In what important way are you different now from the person you were two or three years ago — and what caused that change?
lookingFor: Meaningful personal development with a clear cause — not a vague 'I've matured' claim, but a traceable shift in values, behaviour, or perspective.

bullets: Name a specific and significant change, not a superficial one
Point to the experience or realisation that caused it
Show how that change is visible in how you act now
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Three years ago I cared a lot about being seen as smart — I'd shape arguments to sound impressive rather than to actually be right. A teacher called me out on it in front of a group by asking me to explain a position I'd taken and I couldn't, because I'd taken it for the effect. That was genuinely humiliating. I've spent the time since trying to only say things I can actually defend, and to say 'I don't know' when I don't. It's a slower way to operate in discussion, but I trust my own thinking more because I'm not performing it. The shift wasn't instant — it took about a year before it felt natural — but it's probably the most important change in how I work.

commonMistakes: Naming a change that's too surface-level to be meaningful
Describing growth without a specific cause
A change so dramatic it sounds fabricated
followUps: Is there a part of your earlier self you've let go of that you sometimes miss?
